sentences.text: Kathy, I want to share two things with you which are very important.
There weren't too many people who escaped from graves.
You must be wondering, how come all these thousands of people were piled around?
True, we were surrounded there, that we didn't run, that we didn't do something.
Now remember, among us, maybe half and maybe less were children-- were infants.
If people started running, these children would suffer a terrible death.
Now, this is why we did not run, and this is why we remained very quiet.
I want to tell you in front of me was standing a father with a boy about 10 years old.
And the boy turned to the father and asked him in Yiddish, father, will it hurt?
And the father said, no, child.
And when they shot them, the father was still embracing his son, and the son fell into the grave alive.
You saw this?
Yes.
I heard them and I understood the Yiddish because he asked-- if it [NON-ENGLISH], and I don't know-- didn't know too much Yiddish, but I understood that.
And the second thing I want to tell you, which is extremely important--
What happened, but I just want to bring up another point.

At this grave in Kopyczynce, in the beginning of the summer of 1943 when the ghetto was being canceled, I want to bear witness to this, that at the grave were not only the SS men, but they were the Wehrmacht, ordinary soldiers, and the Luftwaffe pilots were there, killing us.
Because in history, they believed that it was just Nazis-- they called them Nazis.
And not only were they killing us, but they were throwing the babies, and they were shooting, and they were hilarious.
I saw it with my own eyes.
I was at the edge of the grave and--
What happened is, it was my turn to be killed when suddenly I heard a scream-- Alicia Jurman, run, Alicia Jurman, run.
You know, for me, it was like I was already dead, and somebody was calling me from-- from someplace.
And I turned around and I heard, Alicia, Alicia.
It was Milek my friend, Milek He was caught, and he was standing not far from me, and he was also going to be killed.
And suddenly, I heard the commotion with his screaming.
He jumped up to a German and pulled his machine gun and started shooting at the Germans.
And then there was a whole set up-- I mean, it was-- it was like a nightmare starting all over inside the nightmare.
And people started running into the forest because the grave were dug just about where the forest was beginning.
And I-- and I run, and other people were running.
And I didn't know at all what happened.
I heard machine gun.
I heard shooting.
I heard screaming.
And I ran into the forest, and I kept falling and picking myself up and crying, because I was sure that Milek died there.
But you see, Milek didn't-- he also escaped.
I don't know what happened at the grave because I was already in the forest.
First of all, I was-- I needed clothes, and I tried to get some clothes.
And I was just running and hiding in the forest.
And then I got some clothes, which I had to take from-- from grass where it was drying.
And I decided that I will go to Buczacz, that I will not return to the-- to the Kopyczynce ghetto.
Before we left Buczacz, my mother, my little brother, and I, my mother told us, if we are ever separated, that we should not go back to the place where we came from, but return to Buczacz.

We will meet in Buczacz because we know the area, we know the villages, we know the forest, that somehow along the line, we should go back there and not back to Kopyczynce, or not back to wherever we will be.
So I was going to Buczacz, and I was, on the way, as I was going, I was working in the fields, I went out and begged for work.
And this is how I got a little bit of bread, and some potatoes, and some of the sour milk, what they call, it's like buttermilk.
And I came back to Buczacz.
But I didn't know what happened to Milek.
I was sure that Milek died there.
Yeah, and fields.
Fields.
Like weeding the weeds out of potatoes and all the other things that were growing in the ground, and other weeding.
Yes, well, one, there was a girl who thought-- she was quite nasty to me in one of the fields.
I pretended to be a refugee going into the other direction, not going to Buczacz, going from Buczacz.
Because if I was going from Kopyczynce, they could suspect because they knew what was happening, you know.
They probably heard the shooting.
And so I pretended that I was going to join my family.
And sometimes they believed me, sometimes they didn't.
But one of the girls didn't believe me, but she gave me some potatoes, anyway.
I pretend-- I spoke the Ukrainian language perfectly.
I spoke Polish perfectly.

And when I was on the fields there, I used to look mostly on fields where women worked, and I thought well, the women are not going to leave their fields and going to kill me or going to bring me to the police, so I would be fairly safe with them.
But I never stayed more than a few hours, and then I went back into the forest.
And I stayed the night in the forest, and I continued with this little bit of bread, but it took me quite a few days to get back to Buczacz.
I didn't talk much, I just worked.
I just worked.
When I got back to Buczacz, the first thing I did-- we had-- I knew a family, Pisarski.
I went to them and I asked for some food.
And I told them that-- because my mother knew them too, they lived not far from the ghetto.
And I told them, in case my mother or my brother shows up, to tell them that I am in this area.
And it is in-- then I left because they were so afraid for me to be seen there.
First of all, they didn't believe that I was still alive.
They thought that I was some-- some kind of-- some kind of myth, or some-- or some shadow, or something like that.
But I left because I didn't want to endanger them, either.
And so, but my mother found out-- she came back, and she went to them.
And she found that I am in this area.
And then she was searching for me.
Can you imagine searching for somebody in the fields on the far-- in the villages?
This is almost impossible.
And I went into-- into the village there, and I found, in the fields, a little house, a little shack the farmers used that didn't have any windows.
It was a thatched roof, and built out of-- of wood.
And inside, the farmers who worked the field kept their tools.
And when it rains, they-- they stayed over there, or when they had lunch, they could go, or store things.
Nobody ever-- and I found it, and I made a hole, put straw on the top, and I lived there in this place.
And I had some of the food that the Pisarskis gave me, and I lived on it.
And then every-- very early in the morning, I left and I hid in-- in-- in-- in the ravine, or-- or anywhere in the fields so that nobody should find me in this place.
But I was very tired, and I overslept.
And suddenly, one morning I felt like something fell upon me.
And there was a boy on top of me.
He went into the shack, I didn't hear them because I was fast asleep, and he'd stepped on this hole, because it just was straw covering it, and he fell into me.
Anyway, what happened is, this boy and his cousin-- the cousin was Lala, was a girlfriend of mine.
And of course, she recognized me.
But what happened is, when I came back to Buczacz, Milek had given me a ring, [BACKGROUND NOISE] and I hid this ring in the wall.
And I took this ring out, and I was wearing this gold ring.

And he wanted that ring, and I didn't want to give it to him, and finally I gave it to him, because I thought, if I don't give it to him, he's going to let everybody know that the Jewish girl is hiding there.

But I think, later on, when I wrote my book, I realized that I gave it-- this ring to him because I didn't want Lala to suffer-- my friend to be angry at him and not-- because I could have taken him on.
We could have fought, and I would have gotten that ring-- didn't have to give him up the ring, but I didn't want her to be sad.
But when they left after they took-- he took the ring from me, they left and on the way, they were talking.
Because that night, I had a dream about my mother, somebody waking up, and it was really my mother.

See, my mother was hidden in the bushes not far from that same field, and she heard the two children talk, and she recognized Lala, because Lala was my friend, and she realized that they were talking about me.
And this is how my mother found me.
I-- I thought that she was a ghost.
I-- I was really frightened.
And I found my mother, and then I was together with my mother.
She told me that they cancelled out the ghetto, that she and Hertzl were going to go to Buczacz.
But Hertzl wanted to go out and see what was happening, and they got separated, but that she believed that Hertzl knew, because she talked to him before that-- that he will go to Buczacz.
Now, Hertzl came to Buczacz, later on I found out.
He came to Buczacz, he was looking for us.
But he was on the street.
He was just 10 years old.
And remember I told you about my friend, Slavka?
Now, she had a brother, Bohdan.
They had a cousin who was a Ukrainian policeman.
Now, Bohdan was Hertzl's friend, and what happened, apparently-- Who told you this?
This is what I surmised from what happened to my brother, because later on, I heard that my brother was killed.
And when I-- after the liberation, when I was with Slavka, Bohdan was-- I heard that he-- he was killed, he was recognized by a friend, but I didn't know who was the friend.
And it turned out to be it was Slavka's brother, who was working with his Ukrainian cousin.
And they caught him, and the Ukrainian cousin-- Ukrainian policeman-- killed him.
I found that when I sent Wojciu later on, when I sent somebody to--
OK, my mother--
Now, I found one day I came upon a little shack, a little house, a little shack, and there was a man-- somebody was laying there in front of this house, and I thought it was a dead person.
It was an old man, which later I found out had epileptic seizures.
He was a Polish man, very nice man.
We befriended him.
My mother and I befriended him, and he let us stay in his place.
Now, I went to work every morning into the fields.
I walked 2, and 3, and 4 miles away from that village of [NON-ENGLISH], so and I went to the fields.
I saw people work, and I offered to work there.
And then they gave me something for lunch, and then whatever, if they had a piece of bread leftover, or potatoes, I took them to my mother.
And then another woman came with her two daughters.
And then one day, I was working in the field.
And we were harvesting potatoes, and that was 1943.

And I overheard somebody-- two Ukrainian young boys were working there-- they said-- they were laughing because they are going to-- they found this-- this Jews in-- in the fields, and they're-- they're going to kill them.
Actually, it-- well, no, we weren't harvesting [? potato ?]-- we were weeding potatoes.
We were taking the weeds out, so it was like in middle of the summer, before harvest.
And they say that they-- that they were in-- in-- in that wheat field, and that they are going to let the Ukrainian police know about them, that they're going to kill them.
And they were laughing and things like that.
And they were saying, the wheat field near the mill, there.
So, but, you know, there are many wheat fields.
And then I realized, they are going to be killed.
So the only way I can do is try to find them before they are found and trying to rescue them and take them to safety.
Can you imagine, my trying to find that-- that field?
But when people were hiding, the-- the wheat in Poland is very tall, and when you go into the field, you make tracks because you have to separate the stalks.
And they stay separated because people don't really know how to walk on the field.
And so finally, I found that area that was damaged in the stalks, and I tracked them down.
That was a mother with two children, a boy and a girl.
I had strange feelings when I saw them, because I was angry at them that they have left such tracks that they were tracked down.
I was angry at them because we are all going to die very soon, because I didn't know when they will come to pick them up, but I had tremendous compassion for them.
And then I was mad at the Germans, why they should, you know, try to-- to take them.
And then I was mad at the Ukrainians that they have told the Germans.
It was a whole anger and hatred towards the whole situation, but I [? gotted ?] them away from there.
And I brought them-- and it was daylight, mind you-- I had no choice-- I hid them, and then in the evening, I brought them to Wojciu's house.
And I felt very badly about it, because I was kind of throwing these people without his permission.
Felt very badly.
You see, Kathy, with all that was happening, we children retained our sense of dignity.
We knew we couldn't do things like that, impose on somebody.
We had to have permission.
And I knew I didn't have permission from Wojciu to bring these people-- I brought them anyway.
Oh, he didn't like it at all.
He was very indignant, but I let my mother handle him.
And I was a coward, and I just left for a couple of days, because I was just really afraid that he's going to be angry-- very angry.
But he accepted them.
My mother-- my mother's hair got quite gray.
Her eyes were extremely sad, extremely sad.
But she lived for me.
She-- I don't know where she got the strength to live for me.
It was something that, as an adult, as a mother myself, I can understand her heart was broken, but she had a tremendous amount of love for me.
She knew if she gives up, that I may not be able to survive.
Wojciu.
Oh, he had a Polish name, but I didn't know it at the time.
He was actually later, I found out, from one of the girls that was hidden with us, that he was a Polish nobleman.

He was married to a concert pianist from-- in Paris, and when she died a sudden death, he got ill and he hid in-- in this land, in this little shack that actually belonged to another nobleman, a Polish nobleman.
He resigned from the world.
He didn't have any hair.
He didn't have any teeth.
He had-- he looked like a sun with those beautiful blue eyes.
He was a splendid, beautiful man.
Of course, we had lice, you know?
And whenever I offered to delouse his shirt, he said, Alicia, he called me in Polish, Alicia, are you going to bring more Jews?
I say, Wojciu, you know if I found them, I would bring them.
He says, yes, I know, I know.
You know what was a-- no, they-- they didn't-- I thought that they didn't know, but they did find us.
And I will tell you about it.
It was the winter of 1943.
I had met a very fine Polish girl, Manya, and she had given me a blouse, because I had only one blouse and one skirt made out of a blanket.
And when we had to bathe, we went into this little brook, my mother and I, and we went into it, we took our clothes and washed them, and put them on-- it was bitter cold-- and wrung them out.
You couldn't dry it, we put them on and they had to dry on our bodies.
By the way, while I met him-- I want to tell you about something that happened.
One Sunday, we were late to go and bathe.
Just think about what a beautiful-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
I found my mother in the summer of 1943, and now it was the fall of 1943.
And so we-- we woke up late, and we went to this brook to bathe anyway.
And as I took off my clothes and I rinsed them out, because we didn't have any soap, put them on, and I had to put a rope around me to hold up that skirt, because I was a skeleton.
You could see all my cracked ribs.
And I looked into the water, and I reflect my face.
And all I saw was big eyes, and the rest of us just really, like a skeleton.
And I looked at my mother, and I asked her, Mommy, will I ever be a lady?
And she said to me, Alicia, you are already a lady.
And then she told me the definition-- [INAUDIBLE]'s definition of a lady is a person who is considerate of others.
Nothing to do with jewelry or beautiful clothes.
And then I put my little shirt on, and I said to myself, my mother said I was a lady.
And there were times when I thought I would perish, but I held my head high because my mother said I was a lady, and I had to behave accordingly.
It was--
Yes, it was winter, and this girl had given me this blouse.
And I came Wojciu's.
We heated some snow, and I had just washed my hair and put on the blouse.
It was maybe 9 o'clock at night-- winter.
It was Christmas Eve when suddenly, we heard a knocking on the door.
And the Ukrainian voices, open you damn Jews, I know you are there.
Open the door.
Can you imagine how we felt?
Our hearts stopped beating.
I knew one thing-- that I will protect my mother with my life.
I am going-- not going to see my mother killed.
Anyway, there was a little kitchen where we lived in, and then there was a little room near the door, and there was another room where Wojciu kept some of the straw.
So all of the people went into his room, and they covered them self with straw.
I thought for a moment maybe I should do the same thing, but I decided this is not going to work, close the door.
And I opened the door, and they all filed in-- flew in, you know, because suddenly I opened the door.
And then they say, where is everybody?
And one of the boys stepped forward and looked at me-- and I worked in his parents' fields for a few days, harvested, and I knew him, his name was Petro.
I knew him very well, because we were working and harvesting together.
I wasn't near him, but I was at on end.

And what happened during the time, he had a very beautiful voice, and he was singing a-- from a-- from a-- from a play called It's about a village girl whose sweetheart went away, and she was singing her heart out to him, and then he was answering her.
He was-- and I was in the same play during the Russian occupation.
I was Natalka.
So then he was singing the boy's role, I was answering him as Natalka-- we were singing to each other.
We never took, so-- when he said, Slavka, what are you doing among the Jews?
See, he didn't know I was Jewish.
I told him, I'm a Jew.
And he, you know, was shocked, and then he started clamoring to get a-- so I made my speech.
And I told him, I say, we are just women and children here, innocent people.
And I made my first speech and say, for the crime that you are going to commit here, I say to him, I swear on your God and my God, I will hunt you to the grave.
If you ever have children, you will see my eyes in them in the crime that you will be committing.
You will never be forgiven it.
It will live with you, and our innocent blood will be on you.
And I kept talking and talking.
And our eyes connected, and suddenly, it was very quiet and he motioned them out, and they left us.
And of course, we couldn't stay there anymore, and so I knew some-- some farmers who had barns, and-- and I led-- I knew which would be OK, so I told the people where to go.
I led them there.
And when my mother and some of the people went into that barn, in two barns, I was afraid to go into the barn because I was afraid they will come after us, you know, I was already so fright.
So there was a dog house there, and I climbed-- crawled into that dog house, and I stayed in the dog house, looking out, hope if something will happen so I can go and protect them again.
But the farmer came out, and he wanted his dog to go into dog house, and the dog was barking because he couldn't go into the dog house because I was in the dog house.
The poor dog got such a very bad time, he couldn't go in there.
So I felt badly for the dog.
Anyway, that's the time when I stopped sleeping nights, because I was constantly watching and being afraid that they may come back.
Now, that was-- Where would you be sleeping?
I would not be-- We-- we had to go back to Wojciu.
We had absolutely nowhere to be.
We were just waiting there.
We had nowhere to go, because we couldn't be in the barns.
They found out, they would kill us there.
We went back to Wojciu.
When we-- we went back, I went, during the day.
I was trying to beg for some food and some work, but at night-- I knew they would come, if they come, at night-- I was on watch.
And I thought hopefully that I will be awake, and I will be able to stop them from killing us the second time.
But they never came back.
What happened then, it went into January, and word went around that the Germans are being defeated in the Eastern Front, and that the Russians were coming.
And then one time in February, a contingency of Russian partisans passed that village, and the villagers thought that that was the Russians coming, but they were just partisans.
But it made them afraid to-- to look for Jews, or kill, because they shouldn't pay later on.
Not that anybody paid for killing Jews, but that-- that they assumed that somebody's going to punish them for their murders.
My life continued until March, and then we were liberated in March.
The Russians came into that area.
Very hungry.
I used to eat grass.
And-- and I ate flowers, if there were any flowers blooming, I ate flowers.
I was-- my stomach must've shrunk to-- to very little.
But some people, when I went, I always asked for work first, before I begged.
I used to milk cows, I used to clean the stables, I used to do any-- I-- I-- I did everything possible.
My mother stayed in one place.
You see, my mother, I could never let her go out in the village.
My mother was very, very beautiful.
She had this black hair, white skin, and violet eyes.
Nobody in the village looked like my mother.
It was very obvious that this was not a Polish or Ukrainian woman.
She was just too beautiful.
Not that-- please don't get me wrong, not that Ukrainian and Polish women were not beautiful, she was just not a village woman.
I beg your pardon?
I hid my mother.
I never let her go out.
I brought it to her.
You see, I went back, when I worked in the fields, I went back to sleep in Wojciu's.
So I brought potatoes, and I-- and I brought bread, or whatever milk they gave me.
And if they were kind people, they told me, OK, I asked them, I said, can I come and work in your house?
And they said-- Because they thought when I was working with them that I was a Ukrainian or Polish girl, but they knew eventually that I was a Jewish girl.
Well, I will give you an example.
We had a stove in Poland, in this little shack.
It was made out of stones, and there was a plate, an iron plate, and an opening.
You put the wood, or whatever you had in there, and the plate heated up.
And then you took a potato, and you [? slice ?] very thin pieces.
You put them and baked them.

And then you were so hungry that when your turn came to pick up that little piece of potato, you burned your fingers-- you burnt your tongue because you were so hungry, you couldn't wait for it to cool off.
And then when I lay down, I put my fist in my stomach so that my intestines, when they fell over and they hurt me, they should be stopped.
I was very, very hungry, and when I was given a piece of bread, I never even took a crumb.
I brought it to my mother, my mother divided it up.
And Wojciu used to get some things, so we cooked a soup.
Then we were liberated--
But again, that was in March of 1944.
But I was still not sure what was happening, so my mother and I decided that I would go in first to Buczacz and find out if things were OK.
And then she will come.
And I went in and I met my friend Slavka, and she brought me to her home.
And I was going to sleep with her in her bedroom, but I couldn't sleep on a bed-- wasn't used to sleeping on a bed anymore.
I turned and tossed, and turned and tossed.
What happened is, I went to beg for food, and the people told me that the Russians are here.
See, I didn't know it.
And then I went to look on the street, and on the road, and there they were, traveling.
The Russians were traveling.
What did I-- I-- I-- I can tell you, I was very happy, but remember, there was something very important about us-- that was not a rejoicing, time of rejoicing.

That was a time of facing the truth, because when we were trying to survive, when we're fighting to survive, we were aware of what happened, but we didn't let it into our-- our minds to extent where it will drive us crazy.
But when the liberation came and we were finally free, the tragedy hit us with full power.
Now, I was 13, not even 14 years old when I was liberated, because my birthday was in May, and that was March
